planting.note: Set transplants into a 1:1 mix of peat moss and perlite or pure long-fiber sphagnum moss; never use fertilized potting mix or garden soil, and keep the pot sitting in a tray of rain water or distilled water at all times.

directions: Grow in a nutrient-poor, acidic medium of pure sphagnum moss or a peat-perlite mix kept permanently wet with rain, distilled, or reverse-osmosis water — tap water, fertilizer, and regular potting soil will kill this plant. The deeply cupped pitchers fill with rain and digestive fluid, drowning insects that slip down the waxy inner walls and providing the plant's sole nitrogen source in its naturally sterile bog habitat. Purple pitcher plant is the hardiest of all Sarracenia, tolerating deep freezes across its native range from Labrador to the Gulf Coast, but it needs a cold winter dormancy with reduced light and moisture. Established clumps slowly spread by rhizome and will naturalize in a backyard bog garden or container water feature.

planting.note: Scatter seeds on the surface of moist pure sphagnum or a 1:1 peat-perlite mix without covering — seeds need light to germinate; keep medium saturated with rain or distilled water in a sunny, humid spot.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of live or dried sphagnum moss or a peat-perlite mix kept permanently wet with mineral-free water — seeds are dust-fine and need light to germinate, so do not cover them. Each tiny spoon-shaped leaf is studded with stalked glands tipped with glistening red mucilage that traps and slowly digests gnats and springtails, feeding the plant in the nutrient-starved coastal plain seeps it calls home. Dwarf sundew thrives in full sun with high humidity and will self-sow to form dense colonies when conditions are right. Protect from hard freezes below 20°F, though it resprouts readily from its root crown in milder winters.

planting.note: Float a handful of stems in a sunny pond, water garden, or tub without soil; the plant has no roots and anchors itself loosely to substrate or floats freely — use rain or pond water rather than chlorinated tap water.

directions: Place a clump of stems in a sunny, still or slow-moving water body without adding soil — bladderwort floats free or sprawls loosely across shallow mud and requires no planting medium whatsoever. The threadlike submersed branches bear hundreds of microscopic bladder traps that snap shut in milliseconds when a water flea or copepod brushes a trigger hair, sucking the prey inside to be digested. From summer through fall, cheerful bright-yellow snapdragon-like flowers on slender stalks rise an inch or two above the water surface, making the plant unexpectedly ornamental for a carnivore. It spreads vigorously in warm, nutrient-poor, acidic water and can be thinned by simply pulling out excess growth.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in wet to moist soil along stream banks, pond margins, or low wet areas; hazel alder tolerates periodic flooding and establishes quickly when planted in consistently wet ground.

directions: Plant in consistently wet, moist-to-saturated soil along stream edges, pond margins, or low wet areas in full sun to partial shade; hazel alder is a multi-stemmed native shrub or small tree that thrives where few plants can, colonizing eroded stream banks and stabilizing them with a dense fibrous root system. Like other alders it hosts nitrogen-fixing Frankia bacteria in root nodules, enriching surrounding soil and supporting the broader riparian plant community. Reddish-purple male catkins emerge as early as February before any leaves appear, providing critical early-season pollen for queen bumblebees and other overwintered insects. The persistent woody seed cones persist through winter and are eaten by redpolls, goldfinches, and other seed-eating birds.

planting.note: Scarify seeds lightly with sandpaper or soak overnight in warm water before direct-sowing at the surface of well-drained sandy or loamy soil after the last frost; no thinning needed as plants remain compact.

directions: Direct-sow after the last frost in well-drained, infertile to moderately fertile soil in full sun; rattlebox thrives in dry sandy fields, roadsides, and open woodland edges where it fixes nitrogen through root nodules and improves soil structure with minimal inputs. Bright yellow pea-like flowers appear from midsummer onward, followed by inflated pods that turn black and papery at maturity, causing the loose seeds inside to rattle audibly in the wind — giving the plant its common name. It is an important larval host plant for the ceraunus blue butterfly and several native moth species. Allow plants to set seed and self-sow freely for a self-renewing colony; the entire plant is toxic if ingested by livestock or humans.
